topic: Why a timer waters your garden wrong (and this doesn't)
voiceover_script: A watering timer doesn't know if it rained yesterday. This system does. Set a timer for fifteen minutes twice a day and you'll flood your beds in a wet week and starve them in a heatwave — it waters on a schedule, not on need. This system checks actual soil moisture in each bed with a capacitive sensor, not a clock. Only a bed that's genuinely dry gets watered, and only until it hits its target — different crops, different targets, all handled per bed. The result: every bed gets exactly what it needs, logged to a file so you can actually see what your garden's been doing. It's solar-powered, off-grid, and it won an engineering award. Full plans linked in bio.

scenes.text: A watering timer doesn't know if it rained yesterday. This system does.
Set a timer for fifteen minutes twice a day and you'll flood your beds in a wet week and starve them in a heatwave — it waters on a schedule, not on need.
This system checks actual soil moisture in each bed with a capacitive sensor, not a clock.
Only a bed that's genuinely dry gets watered, and only until it hits its target — different crops, different targets, all handled per bed.
The result: every bed gets exactly what it needs, logged to a file so you can actually see what your garden's been doing.
It's solar-powered, off-grid, and it won an engineering award. Full plans linked in bio.

title_tiktok: Why a timer waters your garden wrong (and this doesn't)
description: Why timer-based watering gets it wrong, and how a soil-sensing Raspberry Pi system fixes it. Full build plans linked in bio.
